key_insight: 7T whole-brain MRSI distinguished motor-cortex neuronal loss from more diffuse glutamatergic dysregulation in ALS, with glutamine-related measures emerging as the most sensitive signal.
clinical_relevance: This suggests a potential imaging biomarker to map ALS heterogeneity, track regional disease burden, and help choose endpoints for future clinical trials.
method: High-resolution whole-brain 7T 3D-CRT-FID-MRSI plus motor-cortex single-voxel sLASER in 5 people with ALS and 7 controls
Bayesian hierarchical mixed-effects models compared tNAA, Glu, Gln, and Glx across regions and over up to 3 sessions per participant
Primary motor cortex was subdivided into bulbar/face, hand/upper-limb, and foot/lower-limb zones to test somatotopic patterns
evidence_signal: At baseline, ALS showed a motor-cortex-specific tNAA/tCr deficit and broader Gln/tCr and Glx/tNAA elevation, with the strongest longitudinal association between metabolite slope and ALSFRS-R domain decline for Glx/tNAA (r=0.82, p<0.001).
watch_item: The cohort was very small and limited to rapidly progressing ALS, so broader validation is needed before this can be used as a robust clinical biomarker.
paper_title: Glutamine and NAA dissociate in ALS across somatotopically defined motor regions using 7T MRSI
